AI Co-Innovation Programme  ·  March 2026
Introducing SENTINEL
Keppel SENTINEL:
Advancing Human-AI Collaboration
with ServiceNow AI Control Tower
A conceptual AI workspace built for Keppel's, grounded in ServiceNow OOTB workflows, real pain points, and a clear principle: AI should act, people should decide.
Sense
Decide
Act
Govern
↓
Concept Approach
How SENTINEL is designed to work
1
Conversational and workspace hybrid
The interface blends an ambient workspace view with Now Assist chat for deeper interaction.
2
Agent-completed work is shown as done
The Ops Manager reads what happened, not what to do.
3
Human approval points are deliberate and minimal
Agents pause only when human oversight is needed — for example, a financial commitment or safety action is required.
4
Agent reasoning is visible
Each completed job can be expanded to show the steps the agent took.
5
Mobile continuity
The front-liner scenario plays out on a mobile app, showing the end-to-end chain from detection to resolution.
↓
Design Principles
The seven qualities of an agentic AI interface that works
How each quality is demonstrated in Keppel SENTINEL
#
Quality
What it means
How it shows in Keppel SENTINEL
1
Adaptable & Dynamic
The interface assembles itself for the task at hand. Static menus and fixed views are replaced by context-generated cards and workflows.
Ops workspace assembles job cards based on what agents completed overnight. No two mornings are identical. The PR approval card only appears when there is a PR to approve.
2
Anticipatory & Personalized
The interface surfaces what hasn't been asked for yet. It reads context and delivers before the worker needs to ask.
User does not search for the anomaly — the AI agent detected it and had already executed the job based on policies. The workspace is pre-populated with completed outcomes, not open tasks.
3
Instantaneous
Speed is not a feature but the psychological precondition for trust. Workers disengage after 10 seconds of waiting.
The demo shows agent thinking steps appearing in real time as tasks are in process. Each step completion is visible as it happens.
4
Multimodal & Ambient
The interface goes where the worker goes. Voice in the field, visual for complex analysis, touch for quick confirmation.
The interface meets both workers where they are — technicians receive their work order as a mobile push notification with a floor map and photo checklist.
5
Transparently Accountable
AI action must be visible and reversible. Workers must be able to see what was done and back out. Calibrated visibility — enough accountability without cognitive overload.
Every AI agent action is logged with timestamp and system source. Ops Manager can expand any card to see the full reasoning trail. But the default view is clean — detail is on demand, not forced.
6
Legibly Reliable
Trust is built through explainability. The visual presentation of information predicts whether workers will trust the system.
The AI agent reasoning panel (Checking BMS… ✓  Cross-referencing SLA… ✓  Preparing briefing…) makes the model's logic legible before it delivers results. Workers can see how the agent reached its conclusion and catch it if it's wrong.
7
Delightful
Delight is the adoption lever that unlocks all six qualities above. Without it, the others go unused. The interface must feel like a colleague, not an ERP system.
The workspace opens with three green cards and one amber. There is no flood of alerts. The visual language is calm and resolved. The Ops Manager's job is to read outcomes and approve one thing — not to manage tasks.
↓
Keppel SENTINEL  ·  AI Control Tower
While your team slept,
SENTINEL was already working.
Between 23:40 and 07:00 on a single night at SG-01, three separate incidents unfolded across people, infrastructure, and physical safety. Every one was detected, acted on, and resolved — before John Tan walked through the door.

What the demo will show  ·  Three scenarios, one morning
1
No approval needed
Staff Coverage
Emergency leave → autonomous reassignment
At 23:40, Amir Bin Hassan submits emergency leave. By 23:52, SENTINEL has already identified two qualified engineers from the A-shift roster, matched their certifications, reassigned both PM tasks, and confirmed via mobile push — all without a single human being interrupted.

John sees one green card at 07:00. The run sheet is unaffected.
ROSTER
LEDGER
2
1 approval tap
Data Center Temperature
BMS detection → field fix → inventory loop
At 04:17, a +1.8°C rising trend on Hall 3's CH-03B chiller triggers GUARDIAN. A field engineer is dispatched, the filter is replaced by 07:15, and STEWARD automatically prepares a replenishment purchase order — ready for John's one-tap approval when he arrives.

Detection to fix: 3h 06m. Three agents. Eight systems. One human tap.
GUARDIAN
DISPATCH
STEWARD
LEDGER
3
No approval needed
Safety Violation
Vision AI detection → classification → resolution
At 03:45, CAM-04 picks up a trolley blocking Row D's emergency egress and a cable bundle on Row F. VIGIL classifies both, cross-references the floor egress map and HSE register, raises a work order with photo evidence, and notifies housekeeping — all within two minutes.

Both issues are cleared and confirmed by 06:50. John sees it resolved at 07:00.
VIGIL
DISPATCH
LEDGER
SENTINEL is not a chatbot. It is not a dashboard. It is the orchestration layer that connects Keppel's existing tools — BMS, HR, scheduling, Vision AI, procurement — and surfaces the right decision to the right person at the right moment. John's job is not to monitor. His job is to lead.
3
Incidents overnight
0
Escalations needed
1
Human decision
▶
Demo begins
Opening Narrative  ·  What SENTINEL did while everyone was asleep
Keppel SENTINEL  ·  SG-01  ·  Monday, 30 March 2026
While John slept,
SENTINEL was watching.
Between 23:40 and 07:00, three separate events unfolded across Keppel's SG-01 data centre — an emergency leave, a rising temperature anomaly, and two safety violations on the floor.

No one was called. No dashboards were checked. No emails were sent. SENTINEL detected each event, reasoned through it, and acted — autonomously. By the time John arrived at 07:00, the night had already been resolved.
3

7h 20m
SENTINEL on watch
S$30M
LD risk exposure protected

Scenario 2  ·  Data Center Temperature

Now Assist — Maintenance Note
Now LLM
Maintenance Closure Note
Unit CH-03B chiller filter (DK-F402) found heavily clogged on inspection. Supply air temp at 24.1°C against 21.0°C baseline (+3.1°C). Filter replaced with new unit drawn from Stockroom A, Bay 4. Post-replacement reading at 07:10: 22.1°C and falling. Unit operating normally. No further action required.
✦
Now Assist — MBR Incident Summary
Now LLM
MBR Entry — March 2026
Thermal anomaly detected autonomously in Hall 3 (SG-01) at 04:17. GUARDIAN flagged +1.8°C trend; field engineer dispatched at 05:50. Root cause: clogged air filter on CH-03B (38 days since last PM). Filter replaced by 07:15. No customer impact. SLA maintained. LD exposure: zero.

Safety Violation — VIGIL-0003
VIGIL Agent · SG-01 CAM-04 · Detected 03:45 · WO dispatched 03:47
WO Dispatched · Housekeeping En Route
VIGIL — What the Agent Did
✦
VIGIL Agent Summary
VIGIL
At 03:45 VIGIL detected two safety issues on the DC floor from CAM-04. Both were classified, cross-referenced against active work orders and the floor egress map, then a combined work order was raised and housekeeping was notified — all within 2 minutes. No human intervention required.
✓
03:45VIGILCAM-04
Trolley detected Row D — stationary >10 min. Cable bundle detected Row F — not in cable tray.
✓
03:46VIGILWork Orders
Cross-ref: no active WO for Rows D or F. Egress map: Row D is designated emergency exit. HSE register: cable-on-floor = mandatory clearance.
✓
03:47VIGILFSM
WO-2026-0894 created. Camera snapshots + floor map location attached. Hassan Kadir (Housekeeping) notified via mobile.
✓
06:50HASSANMobile App
Trolley removed from Row D. Cable bundle rerouted to tray Row F. Photo confirmation submitted. WO closed.
✓
06:52VIGILLEDGER
Camera re-scan confirmed clearance. HSE record created. MBR entry prepared.
